root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

conditional probability and radon-nikodym

conditional probabilities in the discrete case

Definition. Let $X$ be a discrete random variable defined on a probability space $(\Omega, \mfm, P)$. Given $x\in \bbr$ and $E\in \mfm$, we define the conditional probability of $E$ given $X=x$ to be the quotient

\begin{equation} \label{couch-eqn} P(E | X=x) = \frac{P(E, \ X=x)}{P(X=x)}, \end{equation} provided that $P(X=x) >0$. If $P(X=x)=0$, then we (arbitrarily) define the conditional probability to be zero: $P(E|X=x)=0$.

Note that $P(-|X=x)$ is a probability measure on $\Omega$, except at those points $x$ where $P(X=x)=0$. But notice also that the probability that $X$ takes one of these values is $0$, i.e., the preimage set

\begin{equation}\notag X^{-1} \left( \left\{ x\in \bbr : P(X=x) = 0\right\} \right) \end{equation}

in $\Omega$ has probability $0$, precisely because this set is a countable union of sets of probability $0$. Thus, the assignment

\begin{equation}\notag E \mapsto P(E| X=x) \end{equation}

is a probability measure on $\Omega$ for almost all $x\in \bbr$.

Definition. Let $X$ and $Y$ be random variables defined on a probability space $(\Omega, \mfm, P)$ and suppose that $X$ is discrete. We define the conditional expectation of $Y$ given $X=x$ to be the integral

\begin{equation} \label{sofa-eqn} \be(Y | X=x) = \int_\Omega Y(\omega) \ P(\text{d}\omega | X=x), \end{equation} provided that the integral exists and $P(X=x)\neq 0$.

a first step toward general conditional probabilities

Of course, the issue with using \eqref{couch-eqn} as the definition of conditional probabilities in the general scheme is that it might be the case that $P(X=x)=0$ for all $x$, if $X$ is not discrete.

To get around this problem, we may proceed informally as follows. If $X$ is an arbitrary random variable on $(\Omega,\mfm, P)$, $E\in \mfm$, and $[a,b]$ is a closed interval in $\bbr$, we define

\begin{equation}\notag P(E | a \leq X \leq b ) = \frac{P(E, \ a \leq X \leq b)}{P(a \leq X \leq b)}, \end{equation}

in the same spirit as \eqref{couch-eqn}, provided, of course, that the denominator is not $0$. Now, in order to obtain something resembling $P(E|X=x)$, we might hope that the limit

\begin{equation} \label{der-eqn} \lim_{\dev\to 0^+} P(E | x \leq X \leq x + \dev ) = \lim_{\dev\to 0^+}\frac{P(E, \ x \leq X \leq x +\dev)}{P(x \leq X \leq x+\dev)} \end{equation}

exists, and if it does, then it should (intuitively) be a good candidate for the probability $P(E|X=x)$.

But where does one go from here? Does this limit actually exist?

A particularly elegant path forward is to notice that the right-hand side of \eqref{der-eqn} looks an awful lot like some sort of derivative. This leads us to consider…

derivatives of measures

The setting in which we will discuss derivatives of measures involves two finite (positive) Borel measures $\nu$ and $\mu$ on the real line $\bbr$. For all $x\in \bbr$, we define

\begin{equation} \label{derivative-eqn} \frac{\text{d}\nu}{\text{d}\mu}(x) = \lim_{\dev \to 0^+} \frac{\nu\left((x-\dev,x+\dev)\right)}{\mu\left((x-\dev,x+\dev)\right)}. \end{equation}

To make the exposition simpler, I will suppose that this limit exists and is finite almost everywhere (with respect to $\mu$), though one need not actually assume this and instead obtain it as a consequence (see the references below for a more general and careful treatment). Though I will make the explicit link clearer later, a quick glance comparing the right-hand sides of \eqref{der-eqn} and \eqref{derivative-eqn} seems to suggest that we’re investigating the correct object.

By its very definition, the function $\text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$ is a first-order infinitesimal object that only depends on the local behavior of the measures $\nu$ and $\mu$ at a given point $x$. What we want to do, however, is to pass from this local object to global information on the measures. But how do we do this?

For inspiration, let’s turn to a much more familiar situation: let’s consider the familiar derivative

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\text{d} y}{\text{d}x}(x) = f’(x) \end{equation}

that you studied in single-variable calculus. Supposing that $f$ is differentiable everywhere, the classical Mean Value Theorem states that, given a closed interval $[a,b]$, there is a $c\in (a,b)$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag f’(c) = \frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}. \end{equation}

This is a very powerful local-to-global result: indeed, the left-hand side is the derivative evaluated at a single point, while the right-hand side is the average rate of change of $f$ over the entire interval $[a,b]$. If you’ve had a course in elementary real analysis, you know that the Mean Value Theorem is the key to proving the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus relating derivatives (infinitesimal quantities) to integrals (global quantities).

Given a number $\ell\in \bbr$, one immediate corollary of the Mean Value Theorem is that, provided

\begin{equation} \label{mvt-eqn} [a,b] \subseteq \left\{ x\in \bbr : \frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}x}(x) \leq \ell\right\}, \end{equation}

we must have

\begin{equation} \label{measure-eqn} f(b) - f(a) \leq \ell(b-a). \end{equation}

Now, the quantity on the right-hand side is $\ell\lambda(I)$, where $\lambda$ is the Lebesgue measure and $I=[a,b]$. Provided that the function $f$ is non-decreasing and right-continuous, the quantity on the left-hand side is $\lambda_f(I)$, where $\lambda_f$ is the so-called Lebesgue-Stieltjes measure associated with $f$. Intuitively, the function $f$ will “distort” the lengths of intervals, and $\lambda_f$ measures this “distorted” length. The point is that \eqref{measure-eqn} has the form

\begin{equation} \label{words-eqn} (\text{a measure of $[a,b]$}) = \ell \times (\text{a measure of $[a,b]$}), \end{equation}

which is a direct consequence of the Mean Value Theorem.

Now, returning to our measures $\nu$ and $\mu$, the beautiful thing is that an analogous conclusion to \eqref{words-eqn} can be made regarding $\text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$:

Lemma. Let $\nu$, $\mu$, and $\text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$ be as above, and let $0 < \ell < \infty$. If $A$ is a Borel set and

\begin{equation} \label{mvt2-eqn} A \subseteq \left\{ x\in \bbr : \frac{\text{d}\nu}{\text{d}\mu}(x) \leq \ell\right\}, \end{equation}

then

\begin{equation} \label{words2-eqn} \nu(A) \leq \ell \mu(A). \end{equation}

For a proof of this lemma, see the references below.

Notice that \eqref{mvt2-eqn} and \eqref{words2-eqn} are the direct measure-theoretic analogs to \eqref{mvt-eqn} and \eqref{words-eqn}. This theorem is a local-to-global result, which says that if a certain property holds locally on the Borel set $A$, then a global conclusion may be made.

It turns out that this lemma is the key result needed to prove the following important theorem. The definition of absolute continuity will be explained in the course of the proof.

Theorem. Let $\nu$, $\mu$, and $\text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$ be as above. Provided that $\nu$ is absolutely continuous with respect to $\mu$, we have:

  1. The function $\text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$ is well-defined and finite almost everywhere with respect to $\mu$.
  2. The function $\text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$ is measurable.
  3. For all Borel sets $A$, we have \begin{equation}\label{radon-nik-eqn} \nu(A) = \int_A \frac{\text{d}\nu}{\text{d}\mu} \ \text{d}\mu. \end{equation}
  4. If $h$ is any other function that satisfies the equation \eqref{radon-nik-eqn} with $h$ in place of $\text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$, then $h = \text{d}\nu/\text{d}\mu$ almost everywhere with respect to $\mu$.

I will leave the proofs of statements (1.), (2.), and (4.) for you to look up in the references cited below—the only thing I want to do is give a brief outline of a proof of (3.), so that you can see how the lemma is used.

Let $A$ be a Borel set and let $t>1$ be a real number. For each $m\in \bbz$, define

\begin{equation} A_m = A\cap \left\{ x\in \bbr : t^m \leq \frac{\text{d}\nu}{\text{d}\mu}(x) < t^{m+1}\right\}. \end{equation}

Now, clearly the $A_m$’s are all pairwise disjoint and

\begin{equation}\label{inc-eqn} A \supseteq \bigcup_{m\in \bbz} A_m. \end{equation}

In order to compute integrals over $A$, we want to sum up integrals over $A_m$ and leverage the lemma; however, there is no reason to believe that the inclusion \eqref{inc-eqn} is an equality in the general case. What we need is for the sets on both sides of \eqref{inc-eqn} to differ on a set of $\mu$-measure zero, which is the same as requiring the set

\begin{equation} \left\{x\in \bbr: \frac{\text{d}\nu}{\text{d}\mu}(x) =0\right\} \end{equation}

to have $\mu$-measure $0$.

derivations of key properties in the discrete case

Let $X$ and $Y$ be two random variables, defined on a common probability space $(\Omega,\mfm, P)$, with values in a discrete measure space $(\calx,\mfa, \mu)$ where $\mu$ is the counting measure and $\calx \subset \bbr$. Given a subset $B\subseteq \calx$ for which $P(Y \in B)\neq 0$, the assignment

\begin{equation}\notag A \mapsto P(X\in A | Y \in B) \overset{\text{def}}{=} \frac{P(X\in A, Y\in B)}{P(Y\in B)} \end{equation}

yields a well-defined probability measure on $\calx$. By rearranging and using the Law of Total Probability, we get

\begin{equation} \label{yuppers-eqn} P(X\in A, Y\in B) = \sum_{y\in B} P(X\in A | Y=y) P(Y=y), \end{equation}

where we define $P(-|Y=y)$ to be any probability measure on $\calx$ if $P(Y=y)=0$. My goal now is to rewrite both sides of this last equality as integrals over the same set on the sample space $\Omega$.

Beginning with the right-hand side of \eqref{yuppers-eqn}, we get

\begin{align} \sum_{y\in B} P(X\in A | Y=y) P(Y=y) &= \int_B P(X\in A | Y=y) P(Y=y) \ \mu(\text{d}y) \notag \\ &= \int_B P(X\in A | Y=y) \ P_Y(\text{d}y) \notag \\ &= \int_{\left\{Y\in B\right\}} P(X\in A | Y= Y(\omega)) \ P(\text{d}\omega), \label{tree-eqn} \end{align}

where $P_Y$ is the pushforward of $P$ along $Y$, while from the left-hand side we get

\begin{equation} \label{book-eqn} P(X\in A, Y\in B) = \int_{\left\{Y\in B \right\}} I_{\left\{X\in A\right\}}(\omega) \ P(\text{d}\omega), \end{equation}

where $I_{\left\{X\in A\right\}}$ is the indicator function of $\left\{X\in A \right\}$. Putting \eqref{tree-eqn} and \eqref{book-eqn} together gives us the first property in:

Properties of Conditional Distributions. The function

\begin{equation} \label{kern-eqn} \mfa \times \Omega \to \bbr, \quad (A,y) \mapsto P(X\in A | Y=Y(\omega)) \end{equation}

satisfies the following properties:

  1. For all $A\in \mfa$ and $E\in \sigma(Y)$, the $\sigma$-algebra in $\Omega$ generated by $Y$, we have \begin{equation} \label{xmas-eqn} \int_{E} I_{\left\{X\in A\right\}}(\omega) \ P(\text{d}\omega) = \int_{E} P(X\in A | Y=Y(\omega)) \ P(\text{d}\omega). \end{equation}
  2. For fixed $\omega$, the function \eqref{kern-eqn} is a probability measure in the variable $A$.
  3. For fixed $A$, the function \eqref{kern-eqn} is a $\sigma(Y)$-measurable function in the variable $\omega$.

With the conditional distribution in hand, we now turn to the expectation of $X$, conditioned on $Y$; recall its definition:

\begin{equation} \label{almost-eqn} \be(X|Y=y) = \sum_{x\in \calx} x P(X=x|Y=y). \end{equation}

Just as I did with the conditional probabilities, I want to write both sides of this equation as an integral over the same set on the sample space $\Omega$.

Multiplying the right-hand side of \eqref{almost-eqn} by $P(Y=y)$ and summing over $y\in B$ gives us

\begin{align} \sum_{y\in B}\sum_{x\in \calx} x P(X=x|Y=y) P(Y=y) &= \sum_{x\in\calx} x P(X=x, Y\in B) \notag \\ &= \sum_{x\in \calx} x P(XI_{\left\{Y\in B\right\}} = x) \notag \\ &= \int_\calx x \ P_{XI_{\left\{Y\in B\right\}}}(\text{d}x) \notag \\ &= \int_{\left\{Y\in B \right\}} X(\omega) \ P(\text{d}\omega), \label{whosit-eqn} \end{align}

while multiplying the left-hand side by $P(Y=y)$ and summing over $y\in B$ gives

\begin{equation} \label{tv-eqn} \sum_{y\in B} \be(X|Y=y)P(Y=y) = \int_{Y\in B} \be(X|Y=Y(\omega)) \ P(\text{d}\omega). \end{equation}

Putting \eqref{whosit-eqn} and \eqref{tv-eqn} together gives us the first property in:

Properties of Conditional Expectations. The function

\begin{equation} \label{kern1-eqn} \Omega \to \bbr, \quad \omega \mapsto \be(X| Y=Y(\omega)) \end{equation}

satisfies the following properties:

  1. For all $E\in \sigma(Y)$, we have \begin{equation} \label{iterated-eqn} \int_{E} X(\omega) \ P(\text{d}\omega) = \int_{E} \be(X|Y=Y(\omega)) \ P(\text{d}\omega). \end{equation}
  2. The function \eqref{kern1-eqn} is a $\sigma(Y)$-measurable function in the variable $\omega$.

Notice, by the way, that by taking $E = \Omega$ in \eqref{iterated-eqn}, we get the Law of Iterated Expectation:

\begin{equation}\notag \be(X) = \be(\be(X|Y)). \end{equation}

Notice also that we can recover \eqref{xmas-eqn} from \eqref{iterated-eqn} by taking $X = I_{\left\{X\in A\right\}}$ in the latter.

the definitions
